    Home › Aircraft › Boeing 767

    Boeing 767

    Boeing · Widebody · In production (freighter/tanker)

    The aircraft that opened the Atlantic to twins.

    Length61.37 m
    Wingspan51.92 m
    Range10,415 km
    MTOW204 t
    Typical seats245
    Built1,330+
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#20 of 30
    3.99 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#12 of 40
    51.0 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#30 of 43
    0.85 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#25 of 43
    3.33 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#14 of 40
    11.4 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#15 of 30
    833 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Boeing 767's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Boeing 76751.92 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #19 of 43 by length → #24 by weight → #21 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length (767-400ER)61.37 m
    Wingspan (-400ER)51.92 m
    Height16.87 m
    MTOW (-400ER)204,116 kg
    Range (-400ER)10,415 km
    Typical seats (-400ER)245 (3-class)
    Engines2 × GE CF6 / PW4000 / RB211
    First flight26 September 1981
    Built1,330+

    Compare it

    Put the Boeing 767 next to something

    vs Ilyushin Il-86 →vs Airbus A330 →
    vs Boeing 757 vs Airbus A330 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousBoeing 757 Next →Boeing 777

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The 767 changed the map more than almost any aircraft: it was the type that proved twin-engine jets could safely cross oceans, pioneering the ETOPS rules in the 1980s that every modern twin now flies under. Before the 767, crossing the Atlantic meant three or four engines; within a decade, it mostly meant a 767.

    Its second act is freight. The passenger fleets are ageing out, but the 767-300F remains the world's default medium freighter — FedEx alone flies well over a hundred — and the line in Everett is still building them, plus the KC-46 Pegasus tanker for the US Air Force, more than four decades after first flight.

    The stretched 767-400ER of 1999, at 61.37 metres with its raked wingtips, is the family's longest and rarest — only Delta and United ever bought it.

    Variants

    The Boeing 767 line

    767-200 / -200ER
    The originals — ETOPS pioneers.
    767-300 / -300ER / -300F
    The best-sellers; the freighter is still in production.
    767-400ER
    The 61.37 m stretch — Delta and United only. Spec shown above.
    KC-46 Pegasus
    The USAF's 767-based tanker — 180+ ordered, in production.
    Operators

    Who flies the Boeing 767

    The top 10 operators by fleet size, of ≈60 worldwide — the rest are aggregated below. Counts marked ≈ are approximate, verified 2026-07-14.

    FXFedEx ExpressThe world's largest 767 fleet — all freighters.≈130→
    USUS Air Force (KC-46)The Pegasus tanker fleet, growing toward 180+.≈90
    5XUPS AirlinesFreighters, with more converting.≈80→ DLDelta Air LinesThe largest passenger operator; sole -400ER operator with United.≈50→
    USAmazon Air / ATSG-ATIE-commerce freighters.≈50
    UAUnited Airlines≈37→
    DEDHL network≈25
    JLJapan Airlines≈25→ 5YAtlas Air≈15→
    CACargojet≈15
    Other operatorsDozens more passenger, charter, freight and government operators worldwide. Counts are fleet-report approximations.≈150

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
